lessons.title: Delving into data science — visualising data
lessons.objectives:
I can say what data science means
I can explain how visualising data helps us spot patterns and trends
I can use a software tool to visualise a data set and find an insight
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: data science · visualisation · insight · infographic · pattern · trend. TA: everyone has the data tool open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — raw data on the board (the starter worksheet shows the raw numbers). Pupils try to read the “story” from numbers alone; they choose tick options (no writing). Do NOT reveal the story — the point is raw numbers are hard to read.
   • Support: choose hard/easy.  • Core: name one thing hard to see in raw numbers.  • Challenge: suggest how to make the story clearer.
2. (5–20) I-do → we-do. Define data science (one sentence). Reveal the SAME data as Minard’s visualisation (the army band thinning = soldiers dying). Show the infographic + a bar chart. We-do the chart card-sort on the activity worksheet (line=over time, bar=compare, pie=parts of a whole).
   • Support: pre-set the chart for them, name it.  • Core: choose their own chart.  • Challenge: visualisation vs infographic. Likely error: pie chart for time → fix-words “pie = parts of ONE whole, not over time.”
3. (20–45) You-do — visualise the TV viewing data in the tool; paste a screenshot + write ONE insight on the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
4. (45–55) Plenary — pupils self-tick the 3 “I can…”; show their chart + one insight. Homework: visualise the planetary data set.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Global data — testing a prediction
lessons.objectives:
I can give examples of where large data sets are used in everyday life
I can choose criteria and use a data set to test a prediction
I can use my findings to support or change my prediction
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: data · prediction · criteria · outlier. TA: everyone has the data tool open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — a phone traffic map on the board (starter worksheet). Pupils choose what the colours mean and where the data comes from (no writing). Big idea: a useful service needs a LOT of data.
   • Support: choose many-phones vs one-phone.  • Core: where does the data come from?  • Challenge: name another data-hungry service.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — examples of big data (traffic, recommendations, weather). We-do the card-sort on the activity worksheet (uses-big-data vs not). Likely error: thinks a calculator sum needs big data → fix-words “big data = MANY pieces over time.”
3. (15–45) You-do — “Where is the best place to live?” Pupils pick criteria (tick all that apply), make a prediction, then use the data tool to test it. Screenshot ONE graph + write what it told them.
   • Support: choose the criterion + show where to click.  • Core: pick 3 criteria, test the prediction.  • Challenge: investigate an outlier (a sharp drop) and say what caused it. TA: prompt only.
4. (45–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; did the data support or change your prediction? Define outlier.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Statistical state of mind — correlation, outliers & the investigative cycle
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what correlation and outliers mean
I can put the steps of the investigative cycle (PPDAC) in order
I can use the investigative cycle on a data set and make a recommendation
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: correlation · outlier · PPDAC · investigative cycle · variable. TA: everyone has the data tool open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — analyse a life-expectancy graph (starter worksheet). Pupils identify a positive correlation (line rising) and outliers (sharp dips at 1871/1918/1940). Choose, then explain why a war/illness year dips.
   • Support: a correlation is two things changing together.  • Core: why is 1918 so low?  • Challenge: delete an outlier or check it first?
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — define correlation + outliers. Introduce PPDAC. We-do the ORDER task on the activity worksheet (Problem→Plan→Data→Analysis→Conclusion). Likely error: Plan/Data swapped → fix-words “you plan BEFORE you collect.”
3. (20–50) You-do — the roller-coaster scenario (no coaster over 350 ft). Pupils pose a measurable question, read the speed-vs-height scatter (positive correlation), make a chart in the tool, screenshot it, and write a recommendation.
   • Support: give a ready question to investigate.  • Core: pose own question + recommend.  • Challenge: say whether the data is enough. TA: prompt only.
4. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; state the recommendation + the data behind it.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Data for action — problem & plan
lessons.objectives:
I can name and order the steps of the investigative cycle
I can work out what data is needed to answer a question
I can make a simple data capture form
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: PPDAC · investigative cycle · data capture · data source. Safety: agree where pupils may go and the rules for handling litter BEFORE collecting. TA: data-capture tool ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the investigative cycle recap (starter worksheet): MATCH each PPDAC step to what you do in it.
   • Support: which step is FIRST?  • Core: which step decides what data you need?  • Challenge: why Plan before Data?
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — the litter problem. Tell the Disney story (a big problem became one precise, measurable question). We-do the card-sort on the activity worksheet (useful data vs not). Likely error: collecting data that can’t answer the question → fix-words “only collect data that helps the question.”
3. (20–45) You-do — Problem step: turn “reduce litter” into a precise question. Plan step: mind-map all the data needed (template provided). Then build a simple data capture form (Forms / spreadsheet); screenshot it.
   • Support: adapt a starter question; tick the data you could collect.  • Core: pose own question + plan the data.  • Challenge: write two questions; predict the answer first. TA: prompt only.
4. (45–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; show the question + the form. Next lesson: collect + clean the data.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Clean it up — data cleansing & visualising
lessons.objectives:
I can explain why data needs cleansing
I can find and fix errors in a data set
I can make a visualisation from a data set
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: data cleansing · error · analysis · visualisation. We are in the Data + Analysis steps of PPDAC. TA: everyone has the data tool open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — spot the problem (starter worksheet): a zoo spreadsheet scanned from paper. Pupils find the meerkat recorded as 3 m (a scan error). Choose error/correct; explain “garbage in, garbage out” with the heights bar chart.
   • Support: data cleansing = checking + fixing.  • Core: what does the saying mean?  • Challenge: delete the 0 kg elephant or check the form first?
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — one wrong value makes a chart lie. We-do the card-sort on the activity worksheet (clean-it vs leave-it). Likely error: deleting a real-but-unusual value → fix-words “check before you delete.”
3. (20–50) You-do — open own litter data, clean it (fix/remove errors), upload to the tool, make a chart answering one question; screenshot it. Self-rate confidence (slider).
   • Support: point to the cells to check; tick the errors.  • Core: clean + chart one question.  • Challenge: decide real vs error values. TA: prompt only; second chart demo at the front for those who need it.
4. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; show cleaned data + one chart. Next lesson: conclusions.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Make a change — analysis & conclusions
lessons.objectives:
I can read a visualisation to find patterns, trends and outliers
I can draw a conclusion from data
I can make a recommendation and report my findings
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: analysis · conclusion · recommendation · pattern · trend · outlier. The end-of-unit assessment also happens this lesson. TA: everyone has the data tool open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what can we learn? (starter worksheet): a litter visualisation (recyclable waste by location) + the raw data table. Pupils read which area has the most recyclable waste (Dining Hall) and the least litter (Technology corridor); note what the chart does NOT explain.
   • Support: a conclusion answers your question using the data.  • Core: what extra info would help?  • Challenge: why is a recommendation from this alone risky?
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — model analysing (adding ‘distance to nearest bin’ answers some questions). We-do the card-sort on the activity worksheet (pattern / trend / outlier). Likely error: confusing pattern with cause → fix-words “the chart shows WHAT, not WHY.”
3. (20–45) You-do — finish own visualisation, draw a conclusion, write a recommendation backed by the data; screenshot the final chart. Self-rate confidence (slider).
   • Support: sentence starters + ‘a recommendation is…’.  • Core: conclusion + recommendation.  • Challenge: is the data reliable enough; what else to collect? TA: prompt only.
4. (45–55) End-of-unit multiple-choice assessment, then the explorer task (local data sites). Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks are routine; no flashing/sound.
